stories.author: Judd Legum
stories.title: ICE is lying about body cams. These documents prove it.
stories.text: On July 13, Joan Sebastian Durán Guerrero, a 25-year-old from Colombia who was legally authorized to work in the US, was fatally shot by an ICE officer in Biddeford, Maine. The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) claims Durán Guerrero “attempted to flee the scene and, fearing for public safety, an officer discharged his weapon.” Durán Guerrero was not the target of the enforcement action and none of the ICE officers involved were using body cams. Durán Guerrero was the eighth person killed in federal immigration enforcement actions during the current Trump administration. In only one instance were federal immigration officers wearing a body cam. Why are ICE officers not equipped with body cams? Shortly after Durán Guerrero was killed, DHS blamed Democrats. According to a DHS statement the “process of purchasing and issuing body-worn cameras to all of our ICE field offices was interrupted by the Democrats’ multiple government shutdowns.” On July 14, answering questions from CNN’s Kaitlan Collins, White House Border Czar Tom Homan had a similar message: COLLINS: Why were the ICE agents not wearing body cameras in Houston or in Maine during these two incidents? TOM HOMAN, BORDER CZA

stories.author: The Lever team
stories.title: The Winklevoss Twins Gave Trump $20 Million After Regulatory Win
stories.text: Last month, Twin cryptocurrency titans Cameron and Tyler Winklevoss and their cryptocurrency company donated more than $20 million in bitcoin to a prominent political action committee supporting President Donald Trump , according to new Federal Election Commission records reviewed by The Lever . The brothers had reportedly been lobbying for regulators to undo the judgment, which included a $5 million penalty. Each brother donated $5 million on June 19, the records show. MAGA Inc. The Gemini Trust Company, the legal name for the twins’ crypto exchange, donated $10 million, records show . “Nothing says thank you after a favorable regulatory or enforcement action like massive super PAC contributions,” said Michael Beckel, Money in Politics Reform director at campaign finance watchdog Issue One. “It's a threat to our democracy when wealthy megadonors and special interests can buy access and influence, get favorable results, and then invest even larger sums hoping for even more favorable treatment. Showering politicians with campaign cash after favorable treatment perpetuates a cycle of cynicism.” The Winklevoss brothers did not respond to a request for comment ahead of publication. Cre

stories.author: Ken Klippenstein
stories.title: After "Political Terrorism" Summit, U.S. Names 43 Americans + Groups
stories.text: Marco Rubio leads “The Resurgence of Political Terrorism” summit last week A new State Department report on Cuba’s “campaign of subversion against the United States” is a virtual government blacklist against left-wing Americans and groups. Linked to last week’s Rubio-led summit on “ political terrorism ,” the report is unusually candid about the government’s targets. “Many of the most significant upheavals in recent American political history — from the George Floyd riots to the rise of Antifa to the explosion of pro-terrorist activism on American college campuses — can be linked, in some way, shape, or form, to Cuban influence,” the report says. As for who these supposed agents of Cuban influence are, the report is remarkably explicit. It names people like Ben Cohen (of Ben & Jerry’s ice cream fame), Mayors Zohran Mamdani and Karen Bass, media figures like Amy Goodman and Hasan Piker, and organizations like the Democratic Socialists of America and the Amazon Labor Union. The implication is clear: Secretary of State Marco Rubio thinks there should be a modern-day blacklist of the political left in America. stories.author: Drop Site team
stories.title: U.S. and Iran trade strikes for 9th day; Ansarallah announces Saudi blockade; House to vote on fusing elements of U.S., Israeli militaries
stories.text: U.S. continues its strikes on Iran over the weekend and into Monday. Iranian retaliatory attacks. U.S. confirms deaths of three service members. Iran suspends MOU commitments. Iran, U.S. signal openness to diplomacy amid ongoing strikes. Iranian energy ministry announces repairs to Jask water facility complete. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi says Iran expected war and prepared retaliation before U.S.-Israeli attack. Iraq seeks alternative oil transport routes. Israeli forces burn and demolish homes in southern Lebanon, despite “ceasefire.” U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio meets Lebanese president in D.C. Israel bombards Gaza over the weekend. Palestinian composer found dead in Gaza City. Israeli attacks in the occupied West Bank. Hamas names Khalil Al-Hayya as new chief. Two-year-old dies amid chickenpox outbreak in Gaza. Report: Israel holds 16 Palestinian doctors in its prisons. Palestinian Journalists Syndicate says eight journalists killed, 467 violations recorded in first half of 2026.
